2021-01-01 12:34:56

1 Monofactorial statistical tests

1.1 Tests with some VPC data

Many of the exercises for this chapter will involve the following data set from a corpus study on the alternation of particle placement that was introduced in Section 1.3.1.

  • a categorical variable CASE, which numbers all cases of the data;
  • a categorical response variable CONSTRUCTION with the constructional choices speakers made: v_do_prt vs. v_prt_do;
  • a categorical predictor variable called MEDIUM stating whether the constructional choice was made in spoken or in written data;
  • a an ordinal predictor variable DO_COMPLX providing the degree of complexity of the direct object: simple < phrasmod < clausmod;
  • a numeric predictor variable DO_LENSYLL representing the length of the direct object (in syllables);
  • a categorical variable called DO_ANIM stating whether the direct object is animate or inanimate;
  • a categorical variable called DO_CONCR stating whether the direct object is abstract or concrete;
  • a categorical variable called PP stating whether a directional PP is following the verb-particle construction.
rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
summary(xvp <- read.delim( # summarize x, the result of loading
   file="../_inputfiles/04_vpcs-ex.csv", # this file
   stringsAsFactors=TRUE)) # change categorical variables into factors
##       CASE          CONSTRUCTION     MEDIUM       DO_COMPLX     DO_LENSYLL         DO_ANIM   
##  Min.   :  1.00   v_do_prt:100   spoken :100   clausmod:  6   Min.   : 1.00   animate  : 27  
##  1st Qu.: 50.75   v_prt_do:100   written:100   phrasmod: 67   1st Qu.: 2.00   inanimate:173  
##  Median :100.50                                simple  :127   Median : 3.00                  
##  Mean   :100.50                                               Mean   : 4.72                  
##  3rd Qu.:150.25                                               3rd Qu.: 6.00                  
##  Max.   :200.00                                               Max.   :31.00                  
##      DO_CONC      PP     
##  abstract: 95   no :167  
##  concrete:105   yes: 33  
##                          
##                          
##                          
##

Prepare the data set for analysis:

xvp$DO_COMPLX <- factor(         # make the new version of xvp$DO_COMPLX
   xvp$DO_COMPLX,                # the old version of xvp$DO_COMPLX w/
   levels=levels(xvp$DO_COMPLX)[ # w/ this order of levels:
      c(3, 2, 1)])               # simple, phrasmod, clausmod
summary(xvp)
##       CASE          CONSTRUCTION     MEDIUM       DO_COMPLX     DO_LENSYLL         DO_ANIM   
##  Min.   :  1.00   v_do_prt:100   spoken :100   simple  :127   Min.   : 1.00   animate  : 27  
##  1st Qu.: 50.75   v_prt_do:100   written:100   phrasmod: 67   1st Qu.: 2.00   inanimate:173  
##  Median :100.50                                clausmod:  6   Median : 3.00                  
##  Mean   :100.50                                               Mean   : 4.72                  
##  3rd Qu.:150.25                                               3rd Qu.: 6.00                  
##  Max.   :200.00                                               Max.   :31.00                  
##      DO_CONC      PP     
##  abstract: 95   no :167  
##  concrete:105   yes: 33  
##                          
##                          
##                          
##

In what follows, I will use this data set for several different exercises. It it important that you pretend that every exercise question is an independent study: Don’t start thinking that testing all these different hypotheses on one and the same data set is the way to go – instead, treat every exercise question as if it was a brand new study for which you collected these data.

1.1.1 The frequency of DO complexity levels

You are suspecting that the distribution of your three levels of DO_COMPLX is not compatible with those of an earlier reference study, where the three levels of DO_COMPLX were obtained this often:

  • simple: 60%;
  • phrasmod: 30%;
  • clausmod: 10%.

Formulate hypotheses, represent the data graphically, compute the required statistical test, and summarize the result(s) briefly.

The above description calls for a non-directional/2-tailed H1:

  • H0: The frequencies of the three levels of DO_COMPLX in your data do not differ significantly from those observed in the reference study (i.e. 0.6 to 0.3 to 0.1): Χ2=0;
  • H1: The frequencies of the three levels of DO_COMPLX in your data differ significantly from those observed in the reference study (i.e. 0.6 to 0.3 to 0.1): Χ2>0.
(COMPLX.obs <- table(xvp$DO_COMPLX))
## 
##   simple phrasmod clausmod 
##      127       67        6
prop.table(COMPLX.obs)
## 
##   simple phrasmod clausmod 
##    0.635    0.335    0.030

We compute the expected frequencies, what we’d see if our 200 complexity values were distributed as in the reference studies:

(COMPLX.exp <- sum(COMPLX.obs) * c(0.6, 0.3, 0.1)) # all >5
## [1] 120  60  20

If we really really feel the need to visualize information as simple as this – recall my discussion of this issue in Section 3.4 – we could do so with, say, a dot chart:

# observed frequencies
dotchart(pch=16, # a dot chart w/ filled circles
   xlab="Frequency", xlim=c(0, sum(COMPLX.obs)), x=COMPLX.obs)
text(                 # plot text
   x=COMPLX.obs,      # at these x-coordinates
   y=seq(COMPLX.obs), # at these y-coordinates
   labels=COMPLX.obs, # namely this text
   pos=1)             # below the point
# expected frequencies/percentages
points(          # plot points
   x=COMPLX.exp, # at these x-coordinates
   y=1:3,        # at these y-coordinates
   pch=16, col="#00000030")
text(             # plot text
   x=COMPLX.exp,      # at these x-coordinates
   y=seq(COMPLX.exp), # at these y-coordinates
   labels=COMPLX.exp, # namely this text
   pos=3, col="#00000030") # above the point
# legend
legend(                    # place a legend into the plot
   x="topright",           # namely in the top left corner
   legend=c("obs", "exp"), # list the levels of SEX
   fill=c("black", "#00000030"), # & connect them to these colors
   ncol=2,                 # make the legend have 2 columns
   bty="n")                # but no box around it

Then, we compute the contributions to chi-squared, the overall chi-squared statistic, the df-value, and the p-value:

(COMPLX.cont2CS <- ((COMPLX.obs-COMPLX.exp)^2)/COMPLX.exp)
## 
##    simple  phrasmod  clausmod 
## 0.4083333 0.8166667 9.8000000
(COMPLX.chisq <- sum(COMPLX.cont2CS))
## [1] 11.025
pchisq(                     # compute the area under the chi-squared curve
   q=COMPLX.chisq,          # for this chi-squared value
   df=length(COMPLX.obs)-1, # at 2 df
   lower.tail=FALSE)        # only using the right/upper tail/side
## [1] 0.004036005

Then, finally, we compute the

(COMPLX.chisqtest <- chisq.test( # make COMPLX.chisqtest the chi-suared test of
   COMPLX.obs,                   # these observed frequencies
   p=c(0.6, 0.3, 0.1)))          # against these expected proportions
## 
##  Chi-squared test for given probabilities
## 
## data:  COMPLX.obs
## X-squared = 11.025, df = 2, p-value = 0.004036
COMPLX.chisqtest$residuals # extract the residuals from COMPLX.chisqtest
## 
##     simple   phrasmod   clausmod 
##  0.6390097  0.9036961 -3.1304952

According to a chi-squared test for goodness-of-fit, the frequencies of the three levels of complexity in the present data are very significantly different from those of the references study (Χ2=11.03, df=2, p≈0.004). [list observed and expected frequencies, show plot] As indicated by the contributions to chi-squared or the residuals, the main source of this significant result is the low(er) frequency of clausally-modified direct objects in the present data set.

1.1.2 The effect of DO concreteness on the choice of construction

You are suspecting that the concreteness of the DO is correlated with the choice of construction such that with concrete DOs CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt will be (more) preferred whereas with abstract DOs CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do will be (more) preferred. Formulate hypotheses, represent the data graphically, compute the required statistical test, and summarize the result(s) briefly.

The above description calls for a directional/1-tailed H1. However, note that, since we’ll have a chi-squared test with df=1 and since the chi-squared distribution is one-tailed only, for a significant result, we now only need to get a p-value of <0.1:

  • H0: The frequencies of the levels of CONSTRUCTION do not vary as a function of the levels of DO_CONC; Χ2=0.
  • H1: The frequencies of the levels of CONSTRUCTION vary as a function of the levels of DO_CONC (in the way hypothesized above); Χ2>0.
(CX.obs <- table(xvp$DO_CONC, xvp$CONSTRUCTION)) # observed frequencies
##           
##            v_do_prt v_prt_do
##   abstract       34       61
##   concrete       66       39
prop.table(CX.obs, 1)                            # observed percentages
##           
##             v_do_prt  v_prt_do
##   abstract 0.3578947 0.6421053
##   concrete 0.6285714 0.3714286
(CX.odds <- (CX.obs[2,2]/CX.obs[2,1])  / # odds
            (CX.obs[1,2]/CX.obs[1,1]))   # ratio
## [1] 0.3293592

There seems to be an effect:

  • the odds of CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do over v_do_prt are 0.591 to 1 (3966) when DO_CONC: concrete;
  • the odds of CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do over v_do_prt are 1.794 to 1 (61/34) when DO_CONC: abstract;
  • the ratio of these two odds is the odds ratio of 0.591/1.794≈0.329, meaning CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do is less preferred when DO_CONC is concrete than when it is abstract (as predicted).

We plot:

mosaicplot(x=t(CX.obs), # a mosaic plot of the transposed table
    xlab="CONSTRUCTION", ylab="DO_CONC", # w/ these axis labels
   col=c("#4A412AFF", "#FFFFB3FF")) # w/ these colors

To manually compute the expected frequencies, we compute the marginal totals:

addmargins(CX.obs)
##           
##            v_do_prt v_prt_do Sum
##   abstract       34       61  95
##   concrete       66       39 105
##   Sum           100      100 200

The two constructions are equally frequent in the complete data set, but if H0 is true, namely that the distribution of the levels of CONSTRUCTION is independent of the levels of DO_CONC, then the two constructions should also be equally frequent within each level of DO_CONC. Therefore, the expected frequencies of both constructions when should be this:

95 *                                   # the instances of DO_CONC: abstract
   prop.table(table(xvp$CONSTRUCTION)) # distributed like both constructions overall
## 
## v_do_prt v_prt_do 
##     47.5     47.5

And, analogously, the expected frequencies of both constructions when DO_CONC is concrete should be this:

105 *                                  # the instances of DO_CONC: concrete
   prop.table(table(xvp$CONSTRUCTION)) # distributed like both constructions overall
## 
## v_do_prt v_prt_do 
##     52.5     52.5

And we then put those together into one nice table using rbind:

addmargins(CX.exp <- rbind( # make CX.exp the combination of these rows:
   "v_do_prt"=  95 * prop.table(table(xvp$CONSTRUCTION)), # row 1 of what we're creating here
   "v_prt_do"= 105 * prop.table(table(xvp$CONSTRUCTION))  # row 2 of what we're creating here
)) # close cbind & addmargins
##          v_do_prt v_prt_do Sum
## v_do_prt     47.5     47.5  95
## v_prt_do     52.5     52.5 105
## Sum         100.0    100.0 200

With those, we can now compute the contributions to chi-squared, the chi-squared value, and the df-value:

(CX.cont2CS <- ((CX.obs-CX.exp)^2)/CX.exp) # contributions to chi-squared
##           
##            v_do_prt v_prt_do
##   abstract 3.836842 3.836842
##   concrete 3.471429 3.471429
(CX.chisq <- sum(CX.cont2CS))              # chi-squared value
## [1] 14.61654
(nrow(CX.obs)-1) * (ncol(CX.obs)-1) # df
## [1] 1

With this, we can now compute the p-value, but remember that we have a directional 1-tailed hypothesis, which is why we compute p as before, but now also divide by 2 (note: this is only possible for chi-squared tests like this with 1 df):

pchisq(                # compute the area under the chi-squared curve
   q=CX.chisq,         # for this chi-squared value
   df=1,               # at 1 df
   lower.tail=FALSE) / # only using the right side
   2                   # & dividing by 2 for 1-tailed test
## [1] 6.58765e-05

Checking this with the function chisq.test:

# note: this function now will return a 2-tailed p-value, not our 1-tailed one:
(CX.chisqtest <- chisq.test(x=CX.obs, # x: the table of observed frequencies
   correct=FALSE))                    # do not apply a correction for continuity
## 
##  Pearson's Chi-squared test
## 
## data:  CX.obs
## X-squared = 14.617, df = 1, p-value = 0.0001318
CX.chisqtest$expected  # like what we computed manually (but this is 2-tailed!)
##           
##            v_do_prt v_prt_do
##   abstract     47.5     47.5
##   concrete     52.5     52.5
CX.chisqtest$residuals # the abstract DOs have slightly higher absolute values
##           
##             v_do_prt  v_prt_do
##   abstract -1.958786  1.958786
##   concrete  1.863177 -1.863177

Finally, it’s usually good to also report some kind of effect. We computed the odds ratio above, now we also compute Cramer’s V:

sqrt(
   CX.chisq /
   (sum(CX.obs) * (min(dim(CX.obs))-1)))
## [1] 0.2703381
# again something you might consider turning into a function ...

[Show the table with the observed and maybe expected frequencies, then:] As per the residuals and as hypothesized, concrete DOs are preferred in the construction v_do_prt and are dispreferred in v_prt_do; the opposite kind of constructional preference is found for abstract objects. According to a (1-tailed) chi-squared test for independence, this correlation is very significant (Χ2=14.62, df=1, p<0.0001), but the effect isn’t particularly strong (V=0.27, odds ratio=0.329 (or log odds ratio=-1.1106)).

1.1.3 The effect of DO length on the choice of construction

You are suspecting that the choice of construction is correlated with the lengths of the DO such that the shorter the DO, the more CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt will be preferred; in other words, the average DO length of CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt will be shorter than that of CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do. Formulate hypotheses, represent the data graphically, compute the required statistical test, and summarize the result(s) briefly.

1.1.3.1 The ‘traditional’ test

The above description calls for a directional/1-tailed H1:

  • H0: The mean DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt is the same as the mean DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do; t=0.
  • H1: The mean DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt is shorter than the mean DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do; t<0.

This scenario would typically call for a t-test for independence/differences, but we begin with the usual descriptive stats and plots:

tapply(                    # apply to
   X=xvp$DO_LENSYLL,       # the DO_LENSYLL-values
   INDEX=xvp$CONSTRUCTION, # a grouping by (the two levels of) CONSTRUCTION
   FUN=function (af) {     # an anonymous function
      c("mean"=mean(af),   # computing the mean
         "var"=var(af),    # & the variance
         "sd"=sd(af))      # & the sd
})                         # & return all
## $v_do_prt
##     mean      var       sd 
## 2.460000 2.917576 1.708091 
## 
## $v_prt_do
##      mean       var        sd 
##  6.980000 24.120808  4.911294

Then, we plot the data and check them for normality, which would be required for the t-test for independent samples:

plot(main="DO_LENSYLL ~ CONSTRUCTION", ylab="Cumulative percentage", # heading & y-axis label
   xlab="Observed values of DO_LENSYLL",  # x-axis label
   xlim=c(0, 31),                         # x-axis limits to allow all observed values
   x=ecdf(xvp$DO_LENSYLL[xvp$CONSTRUCTION=="v_do_prt"]), # v_do_prt first, v_prt_do later
   verticals=TRUE); grid()               # add vertical lines & a grid
lines(ecdf(xvp$DO_LENSYLL[xvp$CONSTRUCTION=="v_prt_do"]), # add (a) line(s) for the ecdf for v_prt_do
   verticals=TRUE, col="grey")                            # add vertical lines & change the color
legend(x=20, xjust=0.5, y=0.1, yjust=0.5, # place legend centered at those coords
   legend=levels(xvp$CONSTRUCTION),       # list the levels of CONSTRUCTION
   fill=c("black", "grey"),               # & connect them to these colors
   ncol=2, bty="n")                       # make the legend have 2 columns, but no box around it

tapply(X=xvp$DO_LENSYLL,     # apply to the DO_LENSYLL values
   INDEX=xvp$CONSTRUCTION,   # a grouping by CONSTRUCTION
   FUN=nortest::lillie.test) # and run lillie.test on each group
## $v_do_prt
## 
##  Lilliefors (Kolmogorov-Smirnov) normality test
## 
## data:  X[[i]]
## D = 0.23615, p-value = 4.519e-15
## 
## 
## $v_prt_do
## 
##  Lilliefors (Kolmogorov-Smirnov) normality test
## 
## data:  X[[i]]
## D = 0.16908, p-value = 1.898e-07

Not normal at all, which means we can actually not use the F-test for variance homogeneity – we will use the Fligner-Killeen test instead (although, if the data are that non-normal, we might already give up on the t-test anyway).

fligner.test(xvp$DO_LENSYLL ~ xvp$CONSTRUCTION)
## 
##  Fligner-Killeen test of homogeneity of variances
## 
## data:  xvp$DO_LENSYLL by xvp$CONSTRUCTION
## Fligner-Killeen:med chi-squared = 44.174, df = 1, p-value = 3.004e-11

Also no variance homogeneity so we have to resort to the U-test. New hypotheses:

  • H0: The median DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt is the same as the median DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do; W=0.
  • H1: The median DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt is shorter than the median DO length of DOs in CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do; W>0.

New descriptive statistics:

tapply(                      # apply to
   X=xvp$DO_LENSYLL,         # the DO_LENSYLL-values
   INDEX=xvp$CONSTRUCTION,   # a grouping by (the two levels of) CONSTRUCTION
   FUN=function (af) {       # an anonymous function
      c("median"=median(af), # computing the median
         "IQR"=IQR(af))      # & the IQR
})                           # & return all
## $v_do_prt
## median    IQR 
##      2      2 
## 
## $v_prt_do
## median    IQR 
##   6.00   6.25

Then, we compute the U-test:

wilcox.test(           # compute a U-test:
   xvp$DO_LENSYLL ~ xvp$CONSTRUCTION, # DO_LENSYLL per CONSTRUCTION
   alternative="less", # level 1 of CONSTRUCTION is < than the 2nd
   correct=FALSE)      # no continuity correction
## 
##  Wilcoxon rank sum test
## 
## data:  xvp$DO_LENSYLL by xvp$CONSTRUCTION
## W = 1509.5, p-value < 2.2e-16
## alternative hypothesis: true location shift is less than 0

According to a 1-tailed U-test, the median length of the DO in CONSTRUCTION: v_do_prt (2, IQR=2) is highly significantly shorter than the median length of the DO in CONSTRUCTION: v_prt_do (6, IQR=6.25): W=1609.5, p<0.00001.

1.1.3.2 A simulation-based test

Your script could be based on the script in Section 4.2.2.1. For a certain number of times, you

  • randomize xvp$CONSTRUCTION (or of course the other variable, xvp$DO_LENSYLL);
  • compute the mean length of the objects for each (now randomized) group of construction tokens;
  • compute the difference the same way as above: ‘v_prt_do minus v_do_prt’ and store it;
  • then determine how many of the stored differences (as a proportion) are the actually observed difference of 6.98-2.46=4.52 or even more extreme; that’s p.
set.seed(sum(utf8ToInt("Tawny Frogmouth"))) # set a replicable random number seed
number.of.iterations <- 15180 # define the number of iterations we will use
diff.value.collector <- rep(NA, number.of.iterations) # define a collector vector
for (i in seq(number.of.iterations)) { # do the following 15180 times:
   # create a randomly-distributed H0 version of xvp$CONSTRUCTION
   CONSTRUCTION.r <- sample(xvp$CONSTRUCTION)
   # compute the difference for this randomized version of the data & collect it in the i-th slot
   diff.value.collector[i] <-
      mean(xvp$DO_LENSYLL[CONSTRUCTION.r=="v_prt_do"]) -
      mean(xvp$DO_LENSYLL[CONSTRUCTION.r=="v_do_prt"])
} # end of for
# evaluation: show the simulated & the observed result
hist(diff.value.collector, xlim=c(-5, 5), breaks=16)
   abline(v=4.52) # a line at the observed result
   text(4.52, 100, "obs", pos=2)

# what's the p-value?
sum(diff.value.collector>=4.52) / # how many differences are >= than ours?
number.of.iterations              # make it a percentage, this is p
## [1] 0
# what's the simulated differences' 95%-CI?
quantile(diff.value.collector, probs=c(0.025, 0.975))
##  2.5% 97.5% 
## -1.18  1.18

According to a simulation test (involving 15180 random pairings of the constructions with the DO lengths), the observed difference in means of 4.52 is highly significantly different from 0: psimulation≈0, the 95%-CI of the simulated differences between means is [-1.18, 1.18], and the maximum simulated difference was 2.82). (In fact, one time I increased the number of simulations by a factor of 100 to 1518000 and there was still not a single occurrence of such a big difference.)

1.2 A correlation in the clause-ordering data

We reload the clause-ordering data:

rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
summary(xco <- read.delim(   # summarize x, the result of loading
   file="../_inputfiles/03_scmc.csv", # this file
   stringsAsFactors=TRUE)) # change categorical variables into factors
##       CASE         ORDER      SCTYPE               CONJ         LENMC            LENSC       
##  Min.   :  1.0   mc-sc:275   caus:199   als/when     : 93   Min.   : 2.000   Min.   : 2.000  
##  1st Qu.:101.5   sc-mc:128   temp:204   bevor/before : 46   1st Qu.: 6.000   1st Qu.: 5.000  
##  Median :202.0                          nachdem/after: 65   Median : 8.000   Median : 8.000  
##  Mean   :202.0                          weil/because :199   Mean   : 9.266   Mean   : 9.362  
##  3rd Qu.:302.5                                              3rd Qu.:11.000   3rd Qu.:12.000  
##  Max.   :403.0                                              Max.   :31.000   Max.   :36.000  
##     LENDIFF          MORETHAN2CL
##  Min.   :-32.00000   no :185    
##  1st Qu.: -4.00000   yes:218    
##  Median :  0.00000              
##  Mean   : -0.09677              
##  3rd Qu.:  3.00000              
##  Max.   : 25.00000

You suspect the lengths of main and subordinate clauses are related. Formulate hypotheses, represent the data graphically, compute the required statistical test, and summarize the result(s) briefly.

The above description calls for a non-directional/2-tailed H1:

  • H0: The main clause lengths and the subordinate clause lengths are not correlated r=0.
  • H1: The main clause lengths and the subordinate clause lengths are correlated r≠0.

1.2.1 The ‘traditional’ test

Normally, we would use Pearson’s r for that, which requires normality of both variables. But are those variables normally distributed?

par(mfrow=c(1, 2)) # make the plotting window have 1 rows & 2 columns
hist(xco$LENMC, xlim=c(0, 40), ylim=c(0, 200),
   main=round(log10(nortest::lillie.test(xco$LENMC)$p.value), 3))
hist(xco$LENSC, xlim=c(0, 40), ylim=c(0, 200),
   main=round(log10(nortest::lillie.test(xco$LENSC)$p.value), 3))
par(mfrow=c(1, 1)) # reset to default

The log10ged p-values are much much less than -1.30103 (=log10(0.05)), the data are not normal at all … Spearman’s ρ it is. We still plot:

qwe <- cor(xco$LENMC, xco$LENSC, method="spearman") # Spearman's rho
par(mfrow=c(1, 2)) # make the plotting window have 1 rows & 2 columns
# perspective 1: LENSC ~ LENMC
plot(pch=16, col="#00000030", # plot filled circles w/ grey transparency
   xlab="Main clause lengths",        xlim=c(0, 35), x=jitter(xco$LENMC), # x-axis stuff
   ylab="Subordinate clause lengths", ylim=c(0, 35), y=jitter(xco$LENSC)) # y-axis stuff
grid(); text(                 # add a grid & plot text
   x=20, y=35,                # at these coordinates
   paste0("Spearman's\nrho=", # this string, followed by
      round(qwe, 3)), pos=1)  # rho rounded to 3 decimals
abline(0, 1, lty=3) # a line for when the lengths are the same
lines(lowess(xco$LENSC ~ xco$LENMC), lwd=3, col="blue")
# perspective 1: LENMC ~ LENSC
plot(pch=16, col="#00000030", # plot filled circles w/ grey transparency
   xlab="Subordinate clause lengths", xlim=c(0, 35), x=jitter(xco$LENSC), # x-axis stuff
   ylab="Main clause lengths",        ylim=c(0, 35), y=jitter(xco$LENMC)) # y-axis stuff
grid(); text(                 # add a grid & plot text
   x=20, y=35,                # at these coordinates
   paste0("Spearman's\nrho=", # this string, followed by
      round(qwe, 3)), pos=1)  # rho rounded to 3 decimals
abline(0, 1, lty=3) # a line for when the lengths are the same
lines(lowess(xco$LENMC ~ xco$LENSC), lwd=3, col="blue")
par(mfrow=c(1, 1)) # reset to default

Let’s compute a p-value for this:

cor.test(
   xco$LENMC,
   xco$LENSC,
   method="spearman",
   exact=FALSE)
## 
##  Spearman's rank correlation rho
## 
## data:  xco$LENMC and xco$LENSC
## S = 9377947, p-value = 0.004776
## alternative hypothesis: true rho is not equal to 0
## sample estimates:
##       rho 
## 0.1403007

Amazing: this is actually still significant! We sum up: Since neither variable was normally distributed, we quantified their correlation with Spearman’s ρ. We found a positive correlation (ρ=0.14), which is significantly greater than 0 (S=9377947, p≈0.0048): longer main clauses are associated with longer subordinate clauses.

1.2.2 A simulation-based test

Your script could be based on the script in Section 4.2.2.1. For a certain number of times, you

  • randomize xco$LENMC (or of course the other variable, xco$LENSC);
  • compute Spearman’s ρ the same way as above and store it;
  • then determine how many of the stored ρs (as a proportion) are the actually observed one of 0.14 or even more different from 0; that’s p.
set.seed(sum(utf8ToInt("Alphatrion"))) # set a replicable random number seed
number.of.iterations <- 10420 # define the number of iterations we will use
rho.value.collector <- rep(NA, number.of.iterations) # define a collector vector
for (i in seq(number.of.iterations)) { # do the following 10420 times:
   # create a randomly-distributed H0 version of xvp$CONSTRUCTION
   LENMC.r <- sample(xco$LENMC)
   # compute the difference for this randomized version of the data & collect it in the i-th slot
   rho.value.collector[i] <- cor(LENMC.r, xco$LENMC, method="spearman")
} # end of for
# evaluation: show the simulated & the observed result
hist(rho.value.collector, xlim=c(-0.5, 0.5), breaks=16)
   abline(v=qwe) # a line at the observed result
   text(qwe, 1000, "obs", pos=4)

# what's the p-value?
sum(abs(rho.value.collector)>=qwe) / # how many differences are >= than ours?
# we're suing abs because we have a 2-tailed H1, both sides count
number.of.iterations                 # make it a percentage, this is p
## [1] 0.004414587
# what's the simulated rho' 95%-CI?
quantile(rho.value.collector, probs=c(0.025, 0.975))
##        2.5%       97.5% 
## -0.09862455  0.09826779

According to a simulation test (involving 10420 random pairings of the main clause and subordinate clause lengths), the observed Spearman’s ρ of 0.14 is very significantly different from 0: psimulation=0.0044 (and thus extremely close to that of the non-parametric test of Spearman’s ρ of 0.0048) and the 95%-CI of the simulated correlations is [-0.099, 0.098].

1.3 The dictation data, part 4

We’re reloading the dictation data discussed in the exercises to Chapter 1, which were collected to address the question whether the numbers of mistakes the students made in a dictation in their native language (e.g. German) is predictive of the number of mistakes the same students made in a dictation in a language you’re learning (e.g. English):

rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
xdi <- read.delim(        # summarize x, the result of loading
   file="../_inputfiles/04_dictation-ex.csv", # this file
   stringsAsFactors=TRUE) # change categorical variables into factors
xdi[c(1:4, 38:44),]       # rows 1 to 4 and 38 to 44 of the data
##    STUDENT GERMAN ENGLISH COURSE
## 1        1      6      10      a
## 2        2      9      15      a
## 3        3      9      12      a
## 4        4      9      15      a
## 38      38     19      23      a
## 39      39     19      26      a
## 40      40     20      24      a
## 41      41     11      27      b
## 42      42     13      33      b
## 43      43     15      35      b
## 44      44     15      35      b

For this question, the data set was properly set up (in the sense of ‘in the case-by-variable’ format):

  • the variable GERMAN is the independent/predictor variable;
  • the variable ENGLISH is the response;
  • the variable COURSE is a potential moderator because it’s at least theoretically possible that the relationship between GERMAN and ENGLISH is not the same in each of the two courses.

You suspect the numbers of mistakes in German and English are positively correlated (if someone struggles with orthography, maybe they’d do so in both languages). Formulate hypotheses, represent the data graphically, compute the required statistical test, and summarize the result(s) briefly.

The above description calls for a directional/1-tailed H1:

  • H0: The numbers of mistakes in German and English are not correlated: r=0;
  • H1: The numbers of mistakes in German and English are correlated: r>0;

Normally, we would use Pearson’s r for that, which requires normality of both variables:

par(mfrow=c(1, 2)) # make the plotting window have 1 rows & 2 columns
hist(xdi$GERMAN, xlim=c(0, 70), ylim=c(0, 20),
   main=round(log10(nortest::lillie.test(xdi$GERMAN)$p.value), 3))
hist(xdi$ENGLISH, xlim=c(0, 70), ylim=c(0, 20),
   main=round(log10(nortest::lillie.test(xdi$ENGLISH)$p.value), 3))
par(mfrow=c(1, 1)) # reset to default

This does not look like we can actually use Pearson;’s r – we use Spearman’s ρ instead. The first plot will, therefore, not involve a linear model regression line, but we are adding something else that hopefully already struck you as noteworthy:

# plot the data
plot(main="ENGLISH ~ GERMAN",
   # everything about the x-axis:
   xlab="Mistakes in German", xlim=c(0, 35), x=xdi$GERMAN,
   # everything about the y-axis:
   ylab="Mistakes in English", ylim=c(0, 70), y=xdi$ENGLISH,
   # use the letters of COURSE as 'points' & add a grid:
   pch=as.character(xdi$COURSE)); grid() # !!!

# compute the correlation coefficient and ...
(corry <- cor(xdi$GERMAN, xdi$ENGLISH, method="spearman"))
## [1] 0.9312792
# ... add it to the plot
text(    # [plot text
   x=5,  # at this x-coordinate
   y=65, # at this y-coordinate\
   # namely the following text:
   paste0("rho=", round(corry, 3)))

It seems that the slopes of GERMAN are not the same in both courses. How can you check that with what you know from Chapter 4?

You could split up the data set in two different parts, one for each course:

xdi.split <- split(
   xdi,
   xdi$COURSE)
xda <- xdi.split$a
xdb <- xdi.split$b

Because then you can compute a separate regression line for each course and plot them both:

summary(m.a <- lm(
   ENGLISH ~ 1 +
   GERMAN,
   data=xda))$coefficients
##             Estimate Std. Error  t value     Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept) 2.539594  1.6291873 1.558810 1.273318e-01
## GERMAN      1.129204  0.1166611 9.679358 8.379241e-12
summary(m.b <- lm(
   ENGLISH ~ 1 +
   GERMAN,
   data=xdb))$coefficients
##             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept) 3.104571 1.56696725  1.981261 5.483199e-02
## GERMAN      2.159972 0.07480431 28.874972 1.847925e-27
# plot the date
plot(main="ENGLISH ~ GERMAN",
   # everything about the x-axis:
   xlab="Mistakes in German", xlim=c(0, 35), x=xdi$GERMAN,
   # everything about the y-axis:
   ylab="Mistakes in English", ylim=c(0, 70), y=xdi$ENGLISH,
   # use the letters of COURSE as 'points' &
   pch=as.character(xdi$COURSE),
   # use different colors for the courses:
   col=c("#FFFFB3FF", "#4A412AFF")[xdi$COURSE])
   grid() # add a grid
# add both regression lines to the plot
abline(m.a, col=c("#FFFFB3FF")); abline(m.b, col="#4A412AFF")

That looks so much better and appears to explain the data much better especially how we now see the difference between the two courses:

  • the slope of GERMAN in COURSE: a is 1.129;
  • the slope of GERMAN in COURSE: b is 2.16.

But now here’s a hugely important question preparing you for Chapter 5: Why is this procedure of splitting the data up, computing separate regression models for each course, and then comparing the slopes of the two courses like that wrong?! (And that even though it is still often done …)

1.4 Grammar test: before vs. after

A colleague of yours wanted to test whether a particular kind of grammar exercise helps improve the performance of foreign language learners in a grammar test. He noted the number of correct answers produced by 10 learners who participated in a grammar test and then had the learners do the grammar exercise whose effect he was interested in. Then, the learners took another test and he again noted the number of correct responses and hoped as well as suspected that the exercise results in a larger number of correct responses. Then he turned to you for help with the analysis showing you the data:

rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
summary(x <- read.delim(   # summarize x, the result of loading
   file="../_inputfiles/04_grmtest-ex.csv", # this file
   stringsAsFactors=TRUE)) # change categorical variables into factors
##     STUDENT          BEFORE        AFTER     
##  Min.   : 1.00   Min.   :1.0   Min.   :4.00  
##  1st Qu.: 3.25   1st Qu.:2.5   1st Qu.:5.25  
##  Median : 5.50   Median :5.5   Median :6.00  
##  Mean   : 5.50   Mean   :4.9   Mean   :6.40  
##  3rd Qu.: 7.75   3rd Qu.:7.0   3rd Qu.:7.75  
##  Max.   :10.00   Max.   :8.0   Max.   :9.00

You formulate the hypotheses:

  • H0: The learner produce the same numbers of correct responses before and after the exercise: the mean of the differences is 0; t=0.
  • H1: The learner produce more correct responses after the exercise: the mean of the differences after - before is larger than 0; t>0.

First thing, you notice that your colleague apparently never read this book or its second edition, because his data frame does not come in the case-by-variable format … So you send him home and have him not come back till he bought himself the book (and copies for all his family and friends) and learned everything in it. While he’s away doing that, you change his data frame into the ‘right’ case-by-variable format:

summary(x.cbv <- data.frame(
   CASE=1:20,
   STUDENT=factor(rep(x$STUDENT, 2)),
   # note that I am fixing the level order to before-after in the next line
   # to make sure that our x-axis below is properly oriented (like a time line)(
   TESTTIME=factor(rep(c("before", "after"), each=10), levels=c("before", "after")),
   CORRECT=c(x$BEFORE, x$AFTER)))
##       CASE          STUDENT    TESTTIME     CORRECT    
##  Min.   : 1.00   1      :2   before:10   Min.   :1.00  
##  1st Qu.: 5.75   2      :2   after :10   1st Qu.:4.00  
##  Median :10.50   3      :2               Median :6.00  
##  Mean   :10.50   4      :2               Mean   :5.65  
##  3rd Qu.:15.25   5      :2               3rd Qu.:7.00  
##  Max.   :20.00   6      :2               Max.   :9.00  
##                  (Other):8

1.4.1 The ‘traditional’ test

You’re considering the t-test for dependent samples so you compute a bunch of descriptive results for the pairwise differences as well as the before and after samples:

c(summary(after.minus.before <-
   x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="after"]  -
   x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="before"]),
   "sd"=sd(after.minus.before))
##     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.       sd 
## -3.00000 -1.00000  0.50000  1.50000  3.75000  8.00000  3.62859
tapply(X=x.cbv$CORRECT,  # apply to the CORRECT-values
   INDEX=x.cbv$TESTTIME, # a grouping by (the two levels of) x$TESTTIME
   FUN=function (af) {   # an anonymous function
      c("mean"=mean(af), # computing the mean
         "var"=var(af),  # & the variance
         "sd"=sd(af))})  # & the sd & return both
## $before
##     mean      var       sd 
## 4.900000 6.322222 2.514403 
## 
## $after
##     mean      var       sd 
## 6.400000 3.377778 1.837873

Can you do a t-test for dependent samples, are the pairwise differences normally distributed?

hist(after.minus.before,
   main=round(log10(nortest::lillie.test(x=after.minus.before)$p.value), 3))

Looks not too bad, so you visualize using the arrow plot that connects every student’s before to the after result:

plot(main="CORRECT ~ TESTTIME", type="n", axes=FALSE, # plot nothing but the heading
   xlab="Test time", xlim=c(0.9, 2.1), x=0,    # x-axis stuff
   ylab="Correct answers", ylim=c(0, 10), y=0) # y-axis stuff
axis(1, at=1:2, labels=levels(x.cbv$TESTTIME)) # lower x-axis w/ levels at tickmarks at 1:2
axis(2); grid() # make a default left y-axis & a grid
arrows(angle=20, length=0.1,               # draw arrows w/ this kind of tip
   x0=1, y0=x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="before"], # from here
   x1=2, y1=x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="after"],  # to here
   col=c("red", "darkgrey", "green")[factor(sign(after.minus.before))]) # color reflects sign of diff
arrows(angle=20, length=0.1, lwd=3,        # draw arrows w/ this kind of tip
   x0=1, y0=mean(x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="before"]), # from here
   x1=2, y1=mean(x.cbv$CORRECT[x.cbv$TESTTIME=="after"]))  # to here

Then, you compute the t-test for dependent samples:

(t.value <- mean(after.minus.before) * sqrt(length(after.minus.before)) /
            sd(after.minus.before))
## [1] 1.307234
(df.value <- length(after.minus.before)-1)
## [1] 9

Is that significant?

pt(                  # compute the area under the t curve
   q=t.value,        # for this t-Value
   df=df.value,      # at our df
   lower.tail=FALSE) # only using the right/upper tail/side,
                     # i.e. go from H0 in the direction of +infinity
## [1] 0.1117711

Here’s the corresponding plot of the t distribution for df=9:

Figure: t-distribution for df=9

And then we check with the function t.test:

t.test(                            # compute a t-test:
   x.cbv$CORRECT ~ x.cbv$TESTTIME, # formula describing response & predictor
   paired=TRUE,                    # make sure it's the dependent-sample t-test
   alternative="less")             # alternative pertains to the 1st level, i.e. "before"
## 
##  Paired t-test
## 
## data:  x.cbv$CORRECT by x.cbv$TESTTIME
## t = -1.3072, df = 9, p-value = 0.1118
## alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is less than 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##       -Inf 0.6034255
## sample estimates:
## mean difference 
##            -1.5

Remember that, if you had used your colleague’s data frame, you would have gotten the same result in one of these two ways:

t.test(                # compute a t-test:
   x$BEFORE, x$AFTER,  # formula describing response & predictor
   paired=TRUE,        # make sure it's the dependent-sample t-test
   alternative="less") # alternative pertains to the 1st-named vector, i.e. "before"
## 
##  Paired t-test
## 
## data:  x$BEFORE and x$AFTER
## t = -1.3072, df = 9, p-value = 0.1118
## alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is less than 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##       -Inf 0.6034255
## sample estimates:
## mean difference 
##            -1.5
t.test(                   # compute a t-test:
   x$AFTER, x$BEFORE,     # formula describing response & predictor
   paired=TRUE,           # make sure it's the dependent-sample t-test
   alternative="greater") # alternative pertains to the 1st-named vector, i.e. "after"
## 
##  Paired t-test
## 
## data:  x$AFTER and x$BEFORE
## t = 1.3072, df = 9, p-value = 0.1118
## alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is greater than 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.6034255        Inf
## sample estimates:
## mean difference 
##             1.5

(However, you never show him this way of computing the results so that he gets it in his head that the case-by-variable format is the way he should always arrange his data …) You sum up: On average, the learners produce 4.9 correct answers before the exercise and 6.4 correct answers after the exercise. [Maybe show plot.] According to a one-tailed t-test for dependent samples, however, this difference is not statistically significant (t=1.3072; df=9; p=0.1118).

1.4.2 A simulation-based test

Your script could be based on the script in Section 4.2.2.1. For a certain number of times, you

  • randomize x.cbv$CORRECT (or of course the other variable, x.cbv$TESTTIME);
  • compute the difference between the means for the (now randomized) groups of before- and after-results and store it;
  • then determine how many of the stored differences (as a proportion) are the actually observed difference of 6.4-4.9=1.5 or even more extreme; that’s p.
set.seed(sum(utf8ToInt("OrionPax"))) # set a replicable random number seed
number.of.iterations <- 8160 # define the number of iterations we will use
diff.value.collector <- rep(NA, number.of.iterations) # define a collector vector
for (i in seq(number.of.iterations)) { # do the following 8160 times:
   # create a randomly-distributed H0 version of x.cbv$TESTTIME
   TESTTIME.r <- sample(x.cbv$TESTTIME)
   # compute the difference for this randomized version of the data & collect it in the i-th slot
   diff.value.collector[i] <-
      mean(x.cbv$CORRECT[TESTTIME.r=="after"]) -
      mean(x.cbv$CORRECT[TESTTIME.r=="before"])
} # end of for
# evaluation: show the simulated & the observed result
hist(diff.value.collector, xlim=c(-4, 4), breaks=16)
   abline(v=1.5) # a line at the observed result
   text(1.5, 1500, "obs", pos=4)

# what's the p-value?
sum(diff.value.collector>=1.5) / # how many differences are >= than ours?
number.of.iterations             # make it a percentage, this is p
## [1] 0.09019608

According to a simulation test (involving 8160 random pairings of the correct responses with the test times), the observed mean difference of correct answers between the two test times (1.5) is not significantly different from 0; psimulation≈0.09.

1.5 A recent overview article

A recent overview article stated the following (problems already highlighted now):

Languages often give speakers choices, for example the choice between: A) the ditransitive (read the children a story), and B) the prepositional dative (read a story to the children) constructions in English. Corpus or experimental data might reveal a pattern such that there is more use of choice A in one environment (X) than in another environment (Y). But is the difference between the measurements of A and B a significant difference? In other words, is there reason to believe that there is a real difference between the frequency of A and B, or might the difference we observe be just a matter of chance (the null hypothesis)? A chi-square test can tell us the probability that the observed difference is significant. Chi-square tests have been used, for example, to test differences between the two English constructions listed above […], the difference between physical and metaphorical understanding of English path versus road […], and the difference in the use of SVO constructions between a child and his mother […]. While a chi-square test can give an overall evaluation of whether there is something significant in a matrix of numbers, the Fisher test is useful when trying to find exactly which of those numbers deviates significantly from the overall distribution of the matrix.

Discuss what’s wrong in there (in terms of statistics). Here goes:

  • “A chi-square test can tell us the probability that the observed difference is significant.” No, a chi-squared test doesn’t tell us “the probability that the observed difference is significant”, it tells us the probability to see the observed difference or anything deviating even more from H0 in the sample if in the population from which the sample was drawn H0 is true. You can see that the above quote – “the probability that something is significant” – is wrong just by recognizing that the p-value of a chi-squared test for a certain table will always be the same, but whether that p-value is significant or not depends on one’s significance threshold value. If, before a study you decide you use p=0.05 as your significance level and then you gather your data and compute a chi-squared test – e.g., like this: chisq.test(matrix(c(10,20,30,20), ncol=2), correct=FALSE) – then the result will be “significant” (p=0.0209213), but if before your study you decided to use p=0.01 as your significance level, then the same chi-squared test would not be significant. The p-value of a statistical test is not the probability whether a result is significant, it’s the probability to get the observed result or something more extreme in your sample when in the population from which you sampled H0 is true.
  • “the Fisher test is useful when trying to find exactly which of those numbers deviates significantly from the overall distribution of the matrix.” No, it isn’t. A Fisher(-Yates exact) test has pretty much the exact same area of application as a chi-squared test. Just like we computed chisq.test(matrix(c(10,20,30,20), ncol=2), correct=FALSE) above, you can compute fisher.test(matrix(c(10,20,30,20), ncol=2)) – you just use the Fisher-Yates exact test when the expected frequencies of your table are too small for the chi-squared test and the test will return a p-value (and maybe an odds ratio, that’s it). To “find exactly which of [the observed frequencies deviate] significantly from the [expected frequencies following from the marginal totals] of the matrix”, you use what I told you in Section 4.1.1.1: the contributions to chi-squared or the residuals, those will tell what is doing what/most, an overall p-value of fisher.test won’t.

2 Programming bits

2.1 A function to compute Cramer’s V on a 2-dim frequency table

Write a function cramersV that takes as input a 2-dimensional frequency table (like in Section 4.1.2.1) and returns Cramer’s V for it.

cramersV <- function (some.2d.table) {
   chisq.of.table <- chisq.test(some.2d.table, correct=FALSE)$statistic
   output <- sqrt(
     chisq.of.table /
        (sum(some.2d.table) * (min(dim(some.2d.table))-1)))
   names(output) <- "Cramer's V"
   return(output)
}

Let’s test it on the data from Section 4.1.2.1:

CONSTRUCTION <- rep(
   x=c("verb-object-particle", "verb-particle-object"),
   times=c(150, 247))
GIVENNESS <- rep(
   x=c("given", "new", "given", "new"),
   times=c(85, 65, 100, 147))
(qwe <- table(CONSTRUCTION, GIVENNESS))
##                       GIVENNESS
## CONSTRUCTION           given new
##   verb-object-particle    85  65
##   verb-particle-object   100 147
cramersV(qwe)
## Cramer's V 
##  0.1572683

And of course you can also test it on the above exercise.

2.2 A function to convert hexadecimal numbers into decimal numbers

This is the 1st of two assignments that are probably a little harder to solve so try them if you feel like it, but don’t despair if you can’t do it (yet). Your mission, should you choose to accept it, is to write a function hex.2.dec that takes as input a string with a hexadecimal number and returns as output the corresponding decimal number:

hex.2.dec <- function (input.string) {
   # make sure all letters are recognized
   input.string <- toupper(input.string)

   # translation 'matrix':
   hex <- c(0:9, LETTERS[1:6]); dec <- 0:15

   # translate hex to decimal using that matrix
   parts.hex <- unlist(strsplit(input.string, ""))
   parts.dec <- dec[match(parts.hex, hex)]

   # generate the exponent for 16
   exponent <- rev(seq(length(parts.dec)))-1

   # compute value
   return(output <- sum(16^exponent * parts.dec))
}

hex.2.dec("0")    # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0x0))
## [1] 0
hex.2.dec("9")    # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0x9))
## [1] 9
hex.2.dec("C")    # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0xC))
## [1] 12
hex.2.dec("20")   # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0x20))
## [1] 32
hex.2.dec("9a6")  # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0x9a6))
## [1] 2470
hex.2.dec("C000") # same as as.numeric(sprintf("%d", 0xc000))
## [1] 49152

2.3 A function to convert decimal numbers into hexadecimal numbers

This is the 2nd of two assignments that are probably a little harder to solve so try them if you feel like it, but don’t despair if you can’t do it (yet). Your mission, should you choose to accept it, is to write a function dec.2.hex that takes as input a decimal number and returns as output the corresponding hexadecimal string:

dec.2.hex <- function (input.number) {
   if (input.number==0) { # check if the input was zero
      output <- "0"
   } else {
      # determine how long the output string will be (to address problems w/ numbers like 256)
      final.length.of.output <- ceiling(log(input.number+1, 16))

      # define translation 'matrix' and define output
      hex <- c(0:9, LETTERS[1:6]); dec <- 0:15
      output=""

      # while the input number hasnt been reduced completely ...
      while (input.number!=0) {
         current.level <- floor(log(input.number, 16)) # determine the exponent of 16
         current.digit <- floor(input.number/(16^current.level))  # determine the multiplier
         output <- paste0(output, hex[which(dec==current.digit)]) # add the hex version of the multiplier
         input.number <- input.number - (current.digit * (16^current.level)) # reduce input number
      } # iterate

      # deal with numbers that divide by multiple of (powers of) 16 without a remainder (like 32 or 256)
      output <- paste0(output, # add to the output
         paste0(rep("0",       # as many 0s as needed
                    final.length.of.output-nchar(output)),
                collapse=""))
   }
   return(output)
}

dec.2.hex(0)     # same as sprintf("%X", 0)
## [1] "0"
dec.2.hex(9)     # same as sprintf("%X", 9)
## [1] "9"
dec.2.hex(12)    # same as sprintf("%X", 12)
## [1] "C"
dec.2.hex(32)    # same as sprintf("%X", 32)
## [1] "20"
dec.2.hex(2470)  # same as sprintf("%X", 2470)
## [1] "9A6"
dec.2.hex(49152) # same as sprintf("%X", 49152)
## [1] "C000"

(Adam Przepiórkowski proposed a solution using the operator %/% (which I didn’t discuss in the book), which also works well and is shorter than the one I propose above.)
